title: Management Summary — Verdict fragment
description: Structured data the LLM writes for the `### Verdict` sub-section. The renderer composes the final markdown (red HTML blockquote + F-NNN linkification) from this data. The LLM never writes raw markdown for this section.

severity.description: Overall posture. 🟢 green = production-ready, 🟡 yellow = acceptable with caveats, 🔴 red = not production-ready.

opening.description: Opening assessment — ONE or TWO short sentences for the product owner, who owns roadmap and budget but is not a security engineer. State the overall business posture and consequence in plain language (who can do what, with what access, to whose data); do not name attack classes or implementation mechanics. Begin with a posture phrase such as 'Not production-ready', 'Production-ready with reservations', or 'Production-ready'. The bullets[] array supplies concrete scenarios, so do not repeat them here. Do NOT enumerate findings or reference F-NNN / T-NNN IDs. Stay at management altitude: no config keys, library names or versions, CVE/CWE numbers, file names or file:line refs, framework-internal symbols, ports, security acronyms, or protocol names. The renderer prepends the severity emoji.

bullets_intro.description: ONE short sentence that introduces the blockquote of worst-case scenarios below. Frames what the reader is about to see ('the attack paths driving this verdict', 'the scenarios behind the not-production-ready rating'). Renders in bold directly above the red HTML blockquote. Do NOT cite individual finding IDs here — the bullets do that. Example: 'Worst-case scenarios behind this verdict — what an attacker could do today:'.

bullets.description: Worst-case attack scenarios, business-language. Rendered as bold bullets inside the red HTML blockquote. Quick depth typically authors 3–5; standard/thorough may emit up to 8 when distinct attack paths warrant it.

title.description: Short, plain-language business-outcome headline, e.g. 'Admin login without a password'. Rendered bold. Do not name an attack technique or implementation detail.

body.description: Exactly one short sentence in PRODUCT-OWNER language: what an attacker achieves and the broad missing protection. Describe a business or operational consequence, not an exploit recipe. FORBIDDEN here (they belong in §8/§7): security acronyms and attack-class names, config keys, library names or version pins, CVE/CWE numbers, file names / paths / file:line refs, framework-internal symbols, port numbers, HTTP-header names, protocol names, code snippets, or backticks. GOOD: 'Anyone can read and change another customer's records because the app does not reliably check ownership.' BAD: 'routes/order.ts passes req.params.id to findOne() without an ownerId clause (CWE-639).'

items.description: Finding (F-NNN) or threat (T-NNN) reference. Provenance only: it is retained to derive the verification status, but is deliberately not displayed in the management summary. Each reference must directly support this scenario; do not add loosely related Critical findings.

closing.description: ONE short plain-language closing assessment — the bottom line / call to action. Rendered after the bullets blockquote. Do NOT reference F-NNN / T-NNN IDs, security acronyms, attack-class names, config keys, libraries, files, or implementation details.
